questions.question: Two beans of type DataSource are registered, and a service injects DataSource without a qualifier. What happens at startup?
choices: Spring picks the first registered bean and continues.
Spring throws NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException. Fix with @Primary on one bean or @Qualifier at the injection point.
Spring randomly picks one and logs a warning.
questions.explanation: Ambiguous injections fail fast with NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException. Resolve by marking one bean @Primary (default winner) or using @Qualifier at the injection point to pick by name.

questions.question: Which Spring scope corresponds to ASP.NET Core's AddScoped (per-HTTP-request lifetime)?
questions.explanation: @Scope("request") creates one instance per HTTP request, matching AddScoped in ASP.NET Core. @Scope("prototype") gives a new instance per injection, which is closer to AddTransient (but rarely the right choice).

questions.question: Why is constructor injection preferred over field injection (@Autowired on a private field) in modern Spring code?
choices: Field injection no longer works in Spring 6.

Constructor injection gives you final fields, immutable state, trivial unit testability (you instantiate the class directly with mocks), and an explicit dependency contract - field injection requires reflection to set test doubles.
Field injection is slower at runtime.
Field injection cannot be used with @Qualifier.
questions.explanation: Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit in the constructor signature, allows final fields, and makes the class testable without Spring (just call the constructor). Field injection requires reflection or a Spring context to populate, complicating tests and obscuring the contract.
